issues.title: arbitrary-send-eth
issues.description:
Escrow.withdrawFunds() (contract.sol#65-71) sends eth to arbitrary user
	Dangerous calls:
	- address(msg.sender).transfer(amount) (contract.sol#69)
issues.recommendation: Add access control and input validation

issues.title: arbitrary-send-eth
issues.description:
Escrow.releaseFunds() (contract.sol#56-62) sends eth to arbitrary user
	Dangerous calls:
	- address(partyA).transfer(amount) (contract.sol#60)
issues.recommendation: Add access control and input validation

issues.title: missing-zero-check
issues.description:
Escrow.constructor(address,address)._partyB (contract.sol#29) lacks a zero-check on :
		- partyB = _partyB (contract.sol#31)
issues.recommendation: Review and apply security best practices

issues.title: missing-zero-check
issues.description:
Escrow.constructor(address,address)._partyA (contract.sol#29) lacks a zero-check on :
		- partyA = _partyA (contract.sol#30)
issues.recommendation: Review and apply security best practices

issues.title: solc-version
issues.description:
Version constraint ^0.8.20 contains known severe issues (https://solidity.readthedocs.io/en/latest/bugs.html)
	- VerbatimInvalidDeduplication
	- FullInlinerNonExpressionSplitArgumentEvaluationOrder
	- MissingSideEffectsOnSelectorAccess.
It is used by:
	- ^0.8.20 (contract.sol#2)
issues.filename:
Version constraint ^0.8.20 contains known severe issues (https://solidity.readthedocs.io/en/latest/bugs.html)
	- VerbatimInvalidDeduplication
	- FullInlinerNonExpressionSplitArgumentEvaluationOrder
	- MissingSideEffectsOnSelectorAccess.
It is used by:

issues.recommendation: Review and apply security best practices

issues.title: reentrancy-unlimited-gas
issues.description:
Reentrancy in Escrow.releaseFunds() (contract.sol#56-62):
	External calls:
	- address(partyA).transfer(amount) (contract.sol#60)
	Event emitted after the call(s):
	- FundsReleased(amount) (contract.sol#61)
issues.recommendation: Review and apply security best practices

issues.title: reentrancy-unlimited-gas
issues.description:
Reentrancy in Escrow.withdrawFunds() (contract.sol#65-71):
	External calls:
	- address(msg.sender).transfer(amount) (contract.sol#69)
	Event emitted after the call(s):
	- FundsWithdrawn(amount) (contract.sol#70)
issues.recommendation: Review and apply security best practices

issues.title: immutable-states
issues.description: Escrow.partyA (contract.sol#11) should be immutable
issues.recommendation: Review and apply security best practices

issues.title: immutable-states
issues.description: Escrow.partyB (contract.sol#12) should be immutable
issues.recommendation: Review and apply security best practices

issues.title: Transaction Order Dependence (SWC 114)
issues.description:
The value of the call is dependent on balance or storage write
This can lead to race conditions. An attacker may be able to run a transaction after our transaction which can change the value of the call
Classification: SWC-114
issues.recommendation: Review SWC documentation

issues.title: Unprotected Ether Withdrawal (SWC 105)
issues.description:
Any sender can withdraw Ether from the contract account.
Arbitrary senders other than the contract creator can profitably extract Ether from the contract account. Verify the business logic carefully and make sure that appropriate security controls are in place to prevent unexpected loss of funds.
Classification: SWC-105
issues.recommendation: Add access control to withdrawal functions

issues.title: unchecked-transfer
issues.description: unchecked-transfer detected
issues.recommendation: Check return values or use SafeERC20

issues.title: unchecked-transfer
issues.description: unchecked-transfer detected
issues.recommendation: Check return values or use SafeERC20

issues.description: GC: [amount] on Event [FundsDeposited] could be Indexed
issues.recommendation: Follow Solidity style guide

stage2_metadata.security_summary:
Contract Type: Custom
Classification Confidence: 90%
Access Control: role_based
Security Features: Standard
Implementation: Custom from scratch
Subtype: escrow

Reasoning: The contract involves holding funds and requires approval from both parties before releasing them, which is characteristic of an escrow system. This does not fit into any standard template like ERC20 or Governor.

classification.reasoning: The contract involves holding funds and requires approval from both parties before releasing them, which is characteristic of an escrow system. This does not fit into any standard template like ERC20 or Governor.
